replies.author: @MrDavid01228
replies.text: @wtfiswiththosehandles One thing to note about Anakin, and all Jedi in the prequels for the most part. They ALL grew up isolated from others. Anakin literally spent his days being trained by Obi-wan and MAYBE one or two other masters on the council. Now, Anakin would have had more experience dealing with people than most of the other Padawans because he came in late and spent many years as a slave in a shop, but those were still his EARLY years. His personality had not really set into stone yet. Now, we enter into the scene a Sith lord, who excels in subtle manipulations (remember, he fooled the entire Jedi Council for decades while being right under their nose). He was able to start working on Anakin's psyche from that SAME young age, being one of the only people who probably interacted with him outside the Temple before he was finally allowed to leave Coruscant to go on missions with Obi-wan. So here we have a young, impressionable kid being exposed probably at least weekly to a master manipulator. Is it REALLY that much of a surprise that he did not see through the schemes until Luke opened his eyes in RotJ?

replies.author: @removebeforeflightgamingth9923
replies.text: @mikewaterfield3599you guys don’t seem to understand anakin’s fall has to do with selfish desires. He is arrogant and prideful. He believes he is above the Jedi and the sith when he is in knightfall mode. Lucas mentioned if he managed to beat obiwan he would’ve turned and killed sidious. The reason he stayed with sidious after padme and his fall was because he wanted to kill sidious to take over and rule the galaxy as he thought he and padme could, he knew he was not strong enough, but at least he could keep an eye on sidious, which is why he asks Luke to help him over throw the emperor and rule the galaxy together as father and son. Most people don’t understand the mythos of star wars and only listen to vague takes on anakin. But if you listen to the messages George puts in the prequels you’d see that anakin never left the fear he had as a small slave boy in tattoine he never escaped that fear until he got the courage to rescue Luke and kill the emperor, only in his last few minutes with his son was his life saved. The reason he was painted as a great hero and the reason we were able to see his fall was because George was trying to show us that anyone could be corrupted, even the chosen one.

@MrDavid01228 I don't buy that. Jedi are not solitary monks, they are "ambassadors and troubleshooters" (Lucas' words, not mine) trained in martial arts. Per this definition they must have as much knowledge and understanding of affairs and other peoples' minds as combat training. Which is actually shown by Jedi masters throughout the movies.
If they wanted to make it seem like Palpatine is some kind of master manipulator, well they failed miserably. He is as subtle as a sledgehammer. It's either that, or Anakin's IQ dropped sharply in his late teens.

replies.author: @MrDavid01228
replies.text: @wtfiswiththosehandles I am not saying they do not have training. But take a look at the training scenes shown in the prequel movies. A bunch of younglings being taught by Yoda, and another group huddled together in fear when Anakin enters the temple at the head of the clone army. This right here tells us a massive story in how Young Jedi were trained, in that they focused SOLELY on the Force in the early years and would not get to the Ambassadorial training until much older. It means that if an unscrupulous manipulator could get to someone while young they could warp the perception of that person with ease. We do not SEE the interactions between Palpatine and Anakin between PM and CW, all we see is Palpatine in his end game. Of COURSE it appears as subtle as a sledgehammer to us as the Audience (especially since we already KNEW Palpatine was a Sith thanks to the Original Trilogy). The thing you need to remember is that Palpatine was a good manipulator, and good at subtly using the force. We cannot really feel the effects of the second aspect as the force was a fictional creation, but if you use it to cloud the minds of others about your actions it makes manipulations a LOT easier. And in the end, Anakin DID see through the manipulations and called out Palpatine for it. If the Jedi Council had not been morons, Anakin would have been present during the arrest, seen Palpatine attack and kill the two other Jedi and known WHY Windu was acting as he was.

Secret Wars was 12 issue run. Around 20 story pages per issue, that would make a 240 page script. Considering that the pages were much more story dense, we can double that to 480 pages.
If one page is one minute of screen time, that's 480 minutes, or 8 hours.
But they want to condense that into two movies: Doomsday and Secret Wars.
And some of the X-men were part of that story, so if they are going to use them, they will need to allocate time to fit them in the universe.
So I am not getting my hopes up.

replies.author: @DanielZenMaster
replies.text:
To me Endgame was the final one. With Spider-Man Far From Home being just an epilogue. 
Sure, No Way Home was okay, but the story was poorly written with Dr Strange all of a sudden acting, like he's a novice, teen sorcerer, who got his hands on a powerful artifact and ignoring all the warning signs - activated it, just to see what happens? Meh. I still liked it to some degree, because it was great to see Tobey, Andrew and Tom teaming up for their last hurrah! But then that was it. 
I have digital versions of movies from Iron Man to No Way Home (with a few obvious skips: like Shang-Chi or Eternals etc.), so I still watch those that made the impact, but I've seen the direction and I don't touch phase 4 and 5. I just consider it a sloppy fan fiction, not a canon and completely ignore it. That's it.
As for Doomsday? Yeah, I'm not giving Disney any money. I'll probably watch it at some point, just to see how it all ends, but it's unlikely I will tear up, it'll be most likely a chuckle with: That's it?

replies.author: @Exquisite_Cadaver
replies.text: @govegmofomakeamuffin6620 Well, I suppose that depends on what you mean by “company”. I tend to think of “company” as a collection of people with the same task or goal. “Corporation” would be the word I would use to describe a business enterprise dedicated to making a profit. “Company” can refer to a group of friends visiting your home. In that instance, their common goal is to socialize and have fun. Military “companies” may take their name from mercenary outfits of the Renaissance, by today’s company commanders generally don’t have a profit incentive on their mission statements. I suppose that’s where I question Marvel and Disney at this point: if their intention is still to make money, or to ONLY make money. Clearly there is a different goal or mission statement amongst many of the people that make up these companies. Based on all the available evidence that I’ve seen at this point, part of that goal appears to be destroying many of the things I care about. If this was helping them make money, perhaps I could forgive it, but they’ve actually been losing money. A “corporation” with responsibilities to shareholders would normally be aghast at such a situation, but for whatever reason these people have decided that MY money isn’t the money they want. Now, I ask you, what’s the difference between my money and someone else’s hard earned dollars? Clearly the difference is me. Apparently my demographic is actually a “problem”, so much so that they need to include narratives deliberately intended to insult and disparage people like me. “Birth of a Nation” wasn’t made JUST to make money, it was made to promote a hateful ideology. Nothing Marvel or Disney has produced in the last 10 years has been any different. Now, if you want to make the argument that the “Birth of a Nation” filmmakers didn’t “hate” a particular group of people, I fear you may have missed the point of the movie. The people being “She-Hulk” are no different.
